does: This paper presents STRIDE-AI, a systematic threat modeling framework designed specifically for generative AI systems that extends the classical STRIDE methodology to address probabilistic rather than deterministic vulnerabilities. The framework establishes a six-phase assessment lifecycle connecting high-level risk standards like NIST AI RMF to technical vulnerability taxonomies such as OWASP LLM Top 10, and operationalizes this through a dedicated web-based tool. The authors validate their approach through black-box testing of a deployed LLM chatbot, demonstrating that their structured assessment methodology reduced the attack success rate from 80% to 15% in their sandbox case study, providing concrete evidence that systematic threat modeling can meaningfully improve AI system security posture.
connects: This work builds directly on decades of software security practice, particularly the STRIDE threat modeling methodology that has become standard in traditional cybersecurity assessment. However, it responds to a critical gap: existing deterministic threat models fail to account for the probabilistic nature of machine learning systems and emerging attack vectors like model inversion, data poisoning, and prompt injection attacks that have become prevalent in generative AI deployments. The framework bridges established governance standards (NIST AI RMF) and emerging technical vulnerability classifications (OWASP LLM Top 10) that arose specifically to address AI-specific risks. It acknowledges industry surveys showing that most AI-deploying organizations lack dedicated security strategies and addresses the rapidly escalating landscape of adversarial attacks against large language models documented in recent research and incident reports.
enables: This framework enables organizations to adopt systematic, structured security assessment practices for generative AI systems at a time when ad-hoc approaches dominate industry practice. By translating abstract AI governance standards into concrete threat modeling exercises operationalized through tooling, it makes threat assessment more accessible and repeatable across teams and deployments. The work points toward standardized security validation practices for AI systems similar to those that matured in traditional software engineering, potentially enabling future requirements for security certification or compliance. It also establishes a foundation for developing more sophisticated automated threat detection and mitigation strategies, and suggests how classical security methodologies can be adapted for emerging AI paradigms, opening pathways for integrating AI security into enterprise security operations and incident response workflows.
